title: End of module: AI as Search — Problem Solving

questions.text: In the five-part formulation of a search problem, which part specifies what state results from taking a given action in a given state?
choices: The goal test
The transition model
The path cost function
The initial state
questions.explanation: The transition model $\text{result}(s,a)$ gives the state reached by taking action $a$ in state $s$. Together with the actions, it defines the whole state space.

questions.text: What is the "frontier" (or "fringe") in a search algorithm?
choices: The set of states that have already been expanded
The set of nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded
The final path from the start state to the goal
The heuristic function used to guide the search
questions.explanation: The frontier holds nodes waiting to be expanded — generated as successors, but not yet processed themselves.

questions.text: Town network: $S$'s neighbours are $A,B$; $A$'s are $C,D,S$; $B$'s are $D,G,S$; $C$'s is $A$; $D$'s are $A,B,G$; $G$'s are $B,D$. Every road costs $1$. Running breadth-first search from $S$ (neighbours considered alphabetically), what is the cost (number of roads) of the path BFS finds to $G$?
questions.explanation: BFS expands S, A, B, C, D, G in that order and finds $S \to B \to G$ — 2 roads. No 1-road path exists since $S$ has no direct road to $G$.

questions.text: On the same town network, in what order does breadth-first search expand the nodes, starting from $S$, before it finds $G$? Answer as six letters separated by commas, e.g. "S,A,B,C,D,G".
questions.explanation: Dequeuing level by level: S first; then its children A, B; then A's new children C, D (B's child G is added to the frontier but not expanded until dequeued); finally D is expanded (no new nodes), and G is dequeued next and passes the goal test. Order: S, A, B, C, D, G.

questions.text: On the same town network, running depth-first search from $S$ (neighbours considered alphabetically, one branch explored fully before backtracking), what is the cost (number of roads) of the path DFS finds to $G$?
questions.explanation: DFS goes S, A, C (dead end, backtrack), D, B, G, taking the path $S \to A \to D \to B \to G$ — 4 roads. This is longer than BFS's optimal 2-road path.

questions.text: When every action has the same cost, which uninformed search strategy is guaranteed to find a path with the fewest possible steps?
choices: Depth-first search
Breadth-first search
Both guarantee this equally
Neither guarantees this
questions.explanation: BFS explores level by level, so the first time it reaches the goal, no shorter path (fewer edges) could exist — every path with fewer edges has already been ruled out.

questions.text: Which of the following are true of depth-first search? (Select all that apply.)
choices: It explores one branch fully before backtracking to try another
It always finds the path with the fewest steps to the goal
It can use less memory than breadth-first search on a wide, shallow tree
Its frontier is a first-in, first-out queue
questions.explanation: DFS commits to a branch and only backtracks once it's exhausted (true), and needs only remember the current path plus unexplored siblings rather than a whole wide frontier (true), so it can use less memory than BFS. It uses a last-in, first-out stack, not a queue, and gives no guarantee of finding the shortest path — indeed it can find a much longer one, as in the worked trace.

questions.text: What is the defining property of an admissible heuristic $h(n)$?
choices: $h(n)$ is always exactly equal to the true cost from $n$ to the goal
$h(n)$ never overestimates the true cheapest cost from $n$ to the goal
$h(n)$ is only calculated after the search has finished
$h(n)$ never underestimates the true cheapest cost from $n$ to the goal
questions.explanation: Admissible means $h(n)$ is always a lower bound (or exact) on the true remaining cost — it never exaggerates how far there is left to go.

questions.text: In A* search, $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. What does $g(n)$ represent?
choices: An estimate of the remaining cost from $n$ to the goal
The exact path cost already paid to reach $n$ from the start
The number of nodes expanded so far in the whole search
The heuristic value evaluated at the goal state
questions.explanation: $g(n)$ is the actual, known cost of the path taken so far to reach $n$; $h(n)$ is the separate, estimated cost still to come.

questions.text: Weighted town network: road costs $S\text{-}A{=}2$, $S\text{-}B{=}5$, $A\text{-}C{=}4$, $A\text{-}D{=}7$, $B\text{-}D{=}2$, $B\text{-}G{=}6$, $D\text{-}G{=}3$; heuristic $h(S){=}7, h(A){=}6, h(B){=}4, h(C){=}9, h(D){=}3, h(G){=}0$. A* expands $S$ first, with $f(S) = g(S) + h(S)$. Compute $f(S)$.
On the same weighted town network and heuristic, what total path cost does A* find for the cheapest route from $S$ to $G$?
questions.explanation: A* expands S, A, B, D, G and returns the path $S \to B \to D \to G$, costing $5+2+3=10$ — the true cheapest cost, since the heuristic is admissible.

questions.text: On the same weighted town network, what is the exact path A* returns from $S$ to $G$? Answer as town letters separated by arrows, e.g. "S->A->G".
questions.explanation: A* finds $S \to B \to D \to G$, cost $10$ — cheaper than the direct-looking $S \to B \to G$ (cost $11$).

questions.text: On the same weighted network, greedy best-first search (which expands using $h(n)$ alone) finds the path $S \to B \to G$ costing $11$, while A* finds $S \to B \to D \to G$ costing $10$. Given that the heuristic used is admissible, what does this show?
choices: Greedy best-first is always optimal, just slower to run
Greedy best-first can be misled by ignoring cost-so-far, and so is not guaranteed optimal, even with a good heuristic
A* and greedy best-first must always return the same path
The heuristic must actually be inadmissible for this to happen
questions.explanation: Greedy best-first ignores $g(n)$ entirely, so it can commit to a route that looks closest by heuristic alone but is not actually cheapest. A*'s use of $g(n)+h(n)$ is exactly what avoids this.

questions.text: Which evaluation function does A* use to choose which frontier node to expand next?
choices: $f(n) = h(n)$ alone
$f(n) = g(n)$ alone
$f(n) = g(n) \times h(n)$
questions.explanation: A* combines the exact cost so far, $g(n)$, with the estimated cost still to come, $h(n)$: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
